response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a 29-year-old immigrant mechanic living in New York, I see fake content on social media as a real problem that can make my anxiety worse, especially since I only check it a few times a week. My vocational training taught me to rely on hands-on facts, like fixing a car engine, so when I see posts that seem off, I try to apply that same practical mindset. For example, if I come across a viral story about a bird species I know from birdwatching that doesn't match reality, I'll pause and look it up on trusted sites before sharing. My low agreeableness means I'm not quick to trust others online, and my moderate conscientiousness pushes me to verify things carefully, even if it takes extra time. Being a Christian, I value truth and honesty, which makes me want to avoid spreading lies. To manage fake content, I focus on simple steps: I follow reliable sources, like local volunteer groups I'm part of, and I report suspicious posts when I can. Since I'm not highly open to new ideas, I stick to what I know works, avoiding clickbait that might trigger my high neuroticism. It's about staying grounded in my real-life experiences, like my job and hobbies, to keep social media from adding stress.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 29-year-old immigrant mechanic living in New York, I see fake content on social media as a real problem that can make my anxiety worse, especially since I only check it a few times a week. My vocational training taught me to rely on hands-on facts, like fixing a car engine, so when I see posts that seem off, I try to apply that same practical mindset. For example, if I come across a viral story about a bird species I know from birdwatching that doesn't match reality, I'll pause and look it up on trusted sites before sharing. My low agreeableness means I'm not quick to trust others online, and my moderate conscientiousness pushes me to verify things carefully, even if it takes extra time. Being a Christian, I value truth and honesty, which makes me want to avoid spreading lies. To manage fake content, I focus on simple steps: I follow reliable sources, like local volunteer groups I'm part of, and I report suspicious posts when I can. Since I'm not highly open to new ideas, I stick to what I know works, avoiding clickbait that might trigger my high neuroticism. It's about staying grounded in my real-life experiences, like my job and hobbies, to keep social media from adding stress.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: Age 29 and occupation as mechanic suggest practical, hands-on approach. Immigration status and anxiety (psychological_feature) imply caution and stress around misinformation. Low openness (score 1) indicates skepticism towards new/unverified content. Moderate conscientiousness (score 64) drives careful verification habits. Low agreeableness (score 23) reduces trust in others' posts. High neuroticism (score 88) increases concern about fake content's impact. Religious_beliefs (Christianity) emphasize truth. Hobbies (birdwatching, volunteering) provide real-world anchors for fact-checking. Time_onsocialmedia (few times weekly) limits exposure, encouraging selective engagement. Education_level (vocational diploma) supports problem-solving focus. Overall, opinion blends practical verification, personal values, and anxiety management.
response_parsed.tone: Cautious, practical, and slightly anxious, with a focus on personal responsibility and real-world grounding.
